regulationSummary.overview: New Jersey has essentially no gold prospecting potential. The state has three distinct geological regions — the Highlands (Precambrian metamorphic rock), the Piedmont (Triassic sedimentary and igneous), and the Coastal Plain (Cretaceous-Quaternary sedimentary) — none of which contain productive gold deposits. New Jersey is famous for franklinite and zincite from the Franklin/Sterling Hill zinc mines, and various minerals from the Highlands region, but gold is not a part of the state's mineral profile.
keyRules: New Jersey does not have a recreational gold prospecting permit system — there are no gold deposits in the state.
Mineral collecting in New Jersey state forests is generally allowed for personal use — contact the specific forest for current rules.
In-stream work in New Jersey is regulated by the NJ Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) under the Flood Hazard Area Control Act and Freshwater Wetlands Protection Act.
New Jersey state parks prohibit mineral collecting — focus on state forest land if rockhounding.
regulationSummary.hpaInfo: Not applicable — there are no gold-bearing streams in New Jersey.
regulationSummary.suctionDredgeStatus: Not applicable — New Jersey has no productive placer gold streams.
regulationSummary.handPanning: Gold panning in New Jersey streams is not a viable activity — the state has no gold-bearing geology. New Jersey rockhounds should focus on the Franklin Mineral Museum for zinc minerals, or wulfenite and other minerals from Highlands localities.
regulationSummary.disclaimer: Regulations change. Verify current rules at the official sources listed below before going out.

officialResources.title: NJ Geological and Water Survey
officialResources.description: State geological survey — New Jersey has no gold-bearing geology; the survey covers zinc, iron, and other minerals.

officialResources.title: NJ State Forests
officialResources.description: Manages New Jersey state forest land — allows personal mineral collecting in some areas.
